network.overall_strategy: This network is literature-constrained and reflects that prebiotic arginine synthesis is unresolved. The densest supported branch is the surface cyanosulfidic route in which HCN, acetylene, NH3, cyanamide, H2S, phosphate-buffered Cu photoredox chemistry, and UV light generate an arginine α-aminonitrile precursor that hydrolyzes to arginine. A second conceptually simple but experimentally incomplete branch is guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide. Additional pathways to the target are therefore represented as distinct network traversals through the same supported hubs rather than as invented unsupported chemistry. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1/C2 feedstocks and pyruvate under Fe-S mineral catalysis, surface chemistry performs cyanosulfidic construction and photochemical cyanamide/guanidine generation, and biochemical/prebiotic settings carry out hydrolysis, concentration, adsorption, and peptide incorporation. Speculative steps are explicitly labeled as such where the literature indicates gaps.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to surface-bound formate/formate-like species.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S minerals are central to prebiotic CO2 fixation models, with greigite proposed as a ferredoxin-like catalyst linking geochemistry to protometabolism; this supplies reduced carbon feeding later surface chemistry (Jordan et al., 2021; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Fe-S mediated further C1 reduction/carboxylation to acetate
reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed reduction and C-C bond formation gives acetate-like products in an acetyl-CoA-analog regime.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate formation from reduced C1 species on iron-sulfur minerals is part of iron-sulfur world and hydrothermal carbon fixation scenarios that are thermodynamically favored near vent interfaces, although kinetic detail remains incomplete (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Magnetite/greigite-assisted hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal setting; reductive carbon fixation network

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive carbon fixation extends C1/C2 pools to α-keto acids including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: α-Keto acids are central metabolic hubs in hydrothermal protometabolism proposals; pyruvate is a reasonable Fe-mineral-derived node connecting hydrothermal carbon fixation to amino acid chemistry, consistent with vent thermodynamic analyses though not arginine-specific (Amend & Shock, 1998; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Iron mineral reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide evolving to magnetite; pyrite surface assistance

reactions.mechanism: Surface-bound imine formation followed by iron-mediated reduction yields alanine from pyruvate and ammonia.
reactions.reasoning: This captures experimentally demonstrated mineral-mediated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine, showing how hydrothermal keto-acid chemistry feeds amino acid inventories that can intersect with later arginine chemistry (Barge et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2024).

reactions.name: UV photochemical formation of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric precursors

conditions.other: anoxic reduced atmosphere analogue; UV irradiation of CH4/NH3/H2O mixtures

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven atmospheric/surface photochemistry forms cyanamide, which is trapped in shallow aqueous environments.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide is a key guanylating and condensing agent with strong prebiotic credentials, formed by UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures and central to cyanosulfidic networks; mineral photocatalytic surfaces plausibly enhance concentration and photochemistry in surface settings (Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide to guanidine

conditions.other: 254 nm UV, pH 8.4-8.5, 7 days to 10 weeks, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oligomerization/rearrangement in ammonium cyanide solutions yields guanidine.
reactions.reasoning: Lohrmann showed guanidine is formed in significant yield from UV-irradiated ammonium cyanide with iron catalyst and is hydrolytically stable, making it an important nitrogen-rich side node even though direct attachment to a C5 backbone remains unresolved (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic cross-coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in phosphate buffer on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: 35°C, anaerobic, UV-driven cyanosulfidic conditions with H2S

reactions.mechanism: Cu photoredox/cyanosulfidic cross-coupling converts HCN and acetylene into the nitrile alkene acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identified acrylonitrile formation as the entry point to the arginine precursor branch within cyanosulfidic protometabolism under 35°C anaerobic UV conditions; Tsanakopoulou and Sutherland later confirmed this branch (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Amination of acrylonitrile to β-aminopropionitrile
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to activated nitrile-alkene gives β-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly included in the Patel/Sutherland arginine precursor logic, converting the simple nitrile backbone into the β-amino nitrile required for later guanylation chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photoreduction of β-aminopropionitrile to β-aminopropionaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the nitrile branch furnishes the corresponding β-amino aldehyde needed for cyanamide addition.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. describe conversion of β-aminopropionitrile-derived material to β-aminopropionaldehyde before efficient cyanamide trapping; the exact operational details remain embedded in the broader cyanosulfidic network but the intermediate is specifically named (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Efficient guanylation of β-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide to arginine precursor branch

conditions.catalyst: cyanamide trapping within Cu/H2S/UV cyanosulfidic network
conditions.other: 35°C, anaerobic, UV-driven, phosphate-buffered, with subsequent photoreduction and HCN addition to compound 46-like α-aminonitrile

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine addition to cyanamide installs the guanidino precursor, and downstream photoreduction/HCN incorporation yields the α-aminonitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. state that the especially efficient reaction of β-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide suggests production of compound 46, the α-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, alongside proline precursor chemistry; this is the strongest experimentally grounded prebiotic arginine branch available (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine α-aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools; prolonged timescale plausible

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the α-aminonitrile precursor into the corresponding α-amino acid arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of α-aminonitriles to amino acids is standard prebiotic logic, and Patel et al. explicitly identify compound 46 as the α-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, though a continuous one-pot demonstration to arginine has not been completed (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.other: concept anchored in Ba(OH)2-mediated guanylation and modern aqueous catalysis; not demonstrated as a complete prebiotic system

reactions.mechanism: The δ-amino group of ornithine attacks cyanamide to form the guanidinium side chain directly.
reactions.reasoning: This is the simplest conceptually supported route to arginine: first demonstrated by Schulze and Winterstein with ornithine plus cyanamide and later shown highly efficient in water under catalytic conditions, but not yet demonstrated under fully prebiotic conditions in a connected network (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis yielding citrulline

conditions.catalyst: UV-assisted HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis on mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolytic rearrangement yields multiple nitrogen-rich products including citrulline and urea-bearing compounds.
reactions.reasoning: Citrulline has been identified in HCN oligomer hydrolysates, giving prebiotic precedent for urea-cycle-adjacent intermediates even though no validated conversion from these materials to arginine is known (Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Cyanate carbamoylation of ornithine to citrulline

conditions.other: hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analogue; cyanate used as carbamoyl source

reactions.mechanism: The terminal amino group of ornithine is carbamoylated by cyanate to give citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: The literature proposes cyanate as a more kinetically stable prebiotic carbamoyl source than carbamoyl phosphate for a hypothetical urea-cycle analogue; this node is included only because the review identifies it as a plausible but unverified route (Juarez et al., 2021; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite adsorption and concentration of arginine
reactions.mechanism: Cation exchange into clay interlayers concentrates and partially protects the guanidinium-bearing amino acid from dilution and photodamage.
reactions.reasoning: Montmorillonite selectively adsorbs arginine 30-50 times more strongly than neutral amino acids, providing a realistic post-synthetic concentration/protection mechanism for any low-yield arginine formed in surface environments (Catalano et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Dry-heating peptide incorporation of clay-bound arginine
reactions.mechanism: Concentrated amino acids on mineral interfaces undergo dehydration-condensation; arginine is represented here as incorporated into emergent peptide chemistry without introducing extra polymer nodes.
reactions.reasoning: Deliquescent minerals and clays enhance peptide-forming wet-dry cycles, and arginine-montmorillonite interlayer complexes have been shown to participate in peptide bond formation under dry heating; this preserves network connectivity from monomer to biochemical relevance (Campbell et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1998).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic arginine-aminonitrile route
pathways.description: The strongest literature-supported path from simple surface feedstocks to arginine through the Patel-Sutherland compound 46 branch.

pathways.name: Cyanamide-enabled cyanosulfidic route with explicit cyanamide photogenesis
pathways.description: Same arginine-aminonitrile branch, but with cyanamide supplied from UV photochemistry before guanylation.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface feedstock supported cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction builds reduced carbon pools that connect environmentally to the surface cyanosulfidic arginine precursor network.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation route
pathways.description: Conceptually simplest two-phase route: prebiotic ornithine plus cyanamide gives arginine directly.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by clay concentration
pathways.description: Direct guanylation of ornithine to arginine, then adsorption/concentration on montmorillonite.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by peptide-context incorporation
pathways.description: Arginine formed by ornithine guanylation is then retained in biochemical wet-dry peptide-forming settings.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with post-synthetic clay capture
pathways.description: Arginine from the compound 46 branch is concentrated and protected on montmorillonite after hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route into biochemical peptide context
pathways.description: Arginine made via the strongest surface route is subsequently incorporated into early peptide chemistry on mineral interfaces.

pathways.name: HCN-rich surface network with guanidine side chemistry supporting arginine-producing branch
pathways.description: Parallel HCN photochemistry generates guanidine while the same HCN pool feeds the cyanosulfidic branch to arginine, illustrating convergent nitrogen chemistry without claiming guanidine itself converts to arginine.

pathways.name: Speculative urea-cycle-analog entry to ornithine/citrulline space with arginine convergence via ornithine guanylation
pathways.description: A literature-mentioned but unverified branch showing that ornithine-centered chemistry could converge with the guanylation solution to reach arginine; included as explicitly speculative.
